responses: I live in Cheyenne.  My life in Wyoming started when I attended UW in 2011.  My wife and I moved to Colorado in 2018, but always knew we would come back to Wyoming.  In 2023, we moved our family to Cheyenne.  We proudly call ourselves Wyoming converts.  I was raised in Parker, CO.

I am a husband, father of three wonderful kids, a teacher, and a strong conservative.  I have been a teacher for eight years and know firsthand many of the issues in our public education system.  My initial degree was in political science and I have experience working as a legislative aide in the Colorado capitol.  I will bring my experience as a teacher, father, and dedicated resident to the position of representative.

I am optimistic for Wyoming.

With education, the legislature got a lot right in its recent recalibration. There are still many issues facing education and I will bring the unique perspective of an educator to the discussion. To provide a robust public option, curriculum needs to be efficacious and knowledge based. School choice and parental rights will be focuses of my policies.

As a legislator, I will fight for our staple industries. That will involve empowering natural gas, oil, and coal extraction as well as enabling the resurgence of uranium.

In conversations with constituents, data centers have been main concerns I have heard and discussed. While the business of Wyoming is business, Mainstreet shouldn't be diminished for the sake of Wall Street or Silicon Valley. I'll work to maintain Wyoming as the best state to start a business in while working with local governments to ensure that the quality of living and water/energy resources aren’t disregarded in the process.

Transparency for medical bills should be continued and further emboldened. The Hospital Price Transparency Act was a step in the correct direction with that regard, but SF 57 took its teeth out.
Healthcare access and affordability is a nuanced issue with no singular or direct answer, but I will further explore expanding telehealth services as well as policy to encourage and educate people regarding HSAs. I will consider policies that would integrate HSAs with telehealth, direct primary care, or wellness incentives—building on recent federal changes.

Nearly half of Wyoming is under federal control.  I support Wyoming lands being in Wyoming hands.  That doesn't mean the full privatization of those lands, but I believe that interests and stakes are greater for those with a closer connection to the land than a bureaucrat in Washington.  With red tape and confusing or redundant regulation, when it comes to management of forests and open spaces, the federal government is not the best steward of lands; local or state governments would have leaner, more direct means of managing the lands in the best interests of Wyomingites.

In my conversations with hopeful constituents, nearly all have expressed appreciation for the reduction of property taxes.  Several also have brought up that property taxes feel arbitrary with home values and thus property taxes increasing often outside of their control.  Who can say to own their land or property if they are continually paying taxes that feel like rent to the government?  I am in favor of maintaining or expanding property tax relief, especially for those on fixed incomes.  And, to address any resulting shortfalls, I will first pursue fiscal management approaches such as requesting an expansive audit for glut or redundant programs and pursue spending restraints like capping the growth of state appropriations or overall spending to population growth plus inflation (PGI). I would not rule out the possibility of tax swapping.

Saying that the government ought to play an active role in facilitating economic sounds nice and, in some circumstances, it might be done altruistically in practice.  However, it can seem as if publicly funded institutions that seek to play an active role in economic development end up choosing winners and losers.  I believe the best role a government can play when it comes to economic development is ensuring that there are no laws or rules that make the playing field uneven for anyone and otherwise getting out of the way and allowing for personal innovation and business growth.

I consider campaign donations as an extension or form of the First Amendment.  A private individual or association of individuals in the form of PACs can choose to use their money to indicate whose voice/what issue they support in the public sphere.  Regarding monitoring campaign donations, I believe that the current system is sufficient but should continue to be quick and accurate in its reporting.  The secretary of state's website allows for the viewing and scrutinizing of donations and expenditures that each campaign receives and makes.

questions: Where do you live currently? How long have you lived there? How long have you lived in Wyoming? Where were you born?
What age will you be on Election Day, Nov. 3, 2026?
Please tell the voters about yourself including your background and qualifications.
What are the biggest challenges and opportunities facing Wyoming today? As a legislator, how would you address them?

We asked WyoFile readers to rank issues that are important to them, and healthcare costs and access topped the list. What can the Legislature do to make healthcare more affordable and accessible to Wyomingites?
Do you support efforts to sell or transfer public land in Wyoming? How do you plan to ensure that state and federal land is managed in the best interest of the people of Wyoming?
No two Wyoming counties are the same when it comes to how they generate revenue. As a legislator, how would you approach the public's desire for property tax relief with local revenue needs?
What role, if any, should the Wyoming Legislature play in economic development?
Explain your values when it comes to campaign donations. Does Wyoming's system for monitoring campaign donations and expenditures need reform?
pageProps.questionnaireIntro:
The answers below were solicited from candidates via a written questionnaire created by WyoFile reporters and editors in June 2026. Responses are presented exactly as submitted, without fact-checking, wordsmithing or editing for grammar, punctuation or spelling errors. 

Legislative candidates were invited to respond to the questionnaire several times by email and by phone. Out of fairness to the candidates who met the deadline, WyoFile will not add responses after the guide's publication.
pageProps.aboutProject:
WyoFile produced this guide with coding and web development by Thomas Musselman, editing by Tennessee Watson and production assistance by Calla Shosh. Contact Tennessee Watson with questions, corrections or suggestions at <tennessee@wyofile.com>.

WyoFile is a Wyoming 501(c)3 nonprofit, independent, member-supported, public-interest news service reporting on the people, places and policy of Wyoming. WyoFile's [donor base](https://wyofile.com/supporters/) is politically diverse, including some donors who are candidates in this year's election. We maintain separation between sources of revenue and editorial direction, decisions and oversight. Acceptance of financial support does not constitute implied or actual endorsement of donors or their products, services or opinions, and does not confer editorial influence, nor does donor support imply any endorsement of WyoFile's reporting, commentary or editorial position.

This material is available for republication by select media outlets. Contact Collaborations Editor Rebecca Huntington at <rebecca@wyofile.com> to learn how.
